data_in.description: methylKit package with dataSim() and calculateDiffMeth() functions available in R environment

data_out.description: methylDiff object containing differential methylation test statistics, p-values, and q-values from the overdispersion-corrected run
Comparison report or figure showing q-value distributions (corrected vs. uncorrected) demonstrating that MN-corrected q-values are higher on average

domain_knowledge: Overdispersion in methylation data arises from biological variation beyond sampling variance; the MN (Methylation-specific Negative Binomial) method accounts for this heterogeneity to produce more conservative p-value and q-value estimates.
The methylBase object is a unified data structure representing methylation calls across multiple samples at the same genomic bases, with coverage and methylation percentage per base.

Q-values (adjusted p-values) control false discovery rate; more stringent correction results in higher q-values for the same raw test statistic, reducing false positives at the cost of statistical power.

The Chi-square test is appropriate for testing differential methylation when sample sizes or site counts are sufficient; it tests whether the observed methylation proportion differences are statistically significant.

dataSim() generates synthetic methylation data with realistic properties (coverage, methylation ratios, replicate structure) to allow reproducible testing and validation of differential methylation workflows.

script_runs: calculateDiffMeth() executes on simulated methylBase with overdispersion='MN' and test='Chisq'

expert_review: expert_review: Compare q-value distributions (histogram, median, mean) from overdispersion='MN' run versus uncorrected baseline run (overdispersion='none' or default); verify that 'MN'-corrected run produces higher q-values on average (more stringent), consistent with vignette claim that overdispersion correction increases test stringency
expert_review: Assess whether observed q-value shift magnitude and direction align with known behavior of Möbius-Nobrega overdispersion correction in differential methylation testing

evidence_snippets.text: [results] Does overdispersion correction in calculateDiffMeth() with overdispersion='MN' produce more stringent statistical tests (higher q-values) compared to uncorrected differential methylation analysis?: 'This scaling parameter also effects the statistical tests and if there is overdispersion correction the tests will be more stringent in general.'

[results] The calculateDiffMeth() function with overdispersion='MN' parameter calculates a scaling parameter φ = X²/(N-P) to adjust variance as φ·n_i·π̂_i·(1-π̂_i), which makes statistical tests more stringent by correcting for variance in excess of binomial expectations and automatically switches from Chisq to F-test.: 'This can be corrected by calculating a scaling parameter φ and adjusting the variance as φ n_i \hat{\pi_i}(1-\hat{\pi_i}). `calculateDiffMeth` can calculate that scaling parameter and use it in'
[methods] methylKit package with dataSim() and calculateDiffMeth() functions available in R environment: 'title: "methylKit: User Guide v`r packageVersion('methylKit')`"'

[intro] methylDiff object containing differential methylation test statistics, p-values, and q-values from the overdispersion-corrected run: 'The calculateDiffMeth() function is the main function to calculate differential methylation'

[intro] Comparison report or figure showing q-value distributions (corrected vs. uncorrected) demonstrating that MN-corrected q-values are higher on average: 'Depending on the sample size per each set it will either use Fisher's exact or logistic regression'

cmd:
library(methylKit); set.seed(42); mBase <- dataSim(replicates=6, sites=1000, treatment=c(0,0,0,1,1,1)); mDiff_corrected <- calculateDiffMeth(mBase, overdispersion='MN', test='chisq'); mDiff_uncorrected <- calculateDiffMeth(mBase, overdispersion='none', test='chisq'); q_corrected <- mDiff_corrected@data$qvalue; q_uncorrected <- mDiff_uncorrected@data$qvalue; cat('Corrected mean q-value:', mean(q_corrected, na.rm=TRUE), '
'); cat('Uncorrected mean q-value:', mean(q_uncorrected, na.rm=TRUE), '
'); pdf('q_value_comparison.pdf'); par(mfrow=c(1,2)); hist(q_corrected, main='MN-Corrected q-values', xlab='q-value', breaks=50); hist(q_uncorrected, main='Uncorrected q-values', xlab='q-value', breaks=50); dev.off(); saveRDS(mDiff_corrected, 'methylDiff_corrected.rds'); cat('Task completed successfully.
')

expected_outputs: methylDiff object containing differential methylation test statistics, p-values, and q-values from the overdispersion-corrected run
Comparison report or figure showing q-value distributions (corrected vs. uncorrected) demonstrating that MN-corrected q-values are higher on average
finding: The calculateDiffMeth() function with overdispersion='MN' parameter calculates a scaling parameter φ = X²/(N-P) to adjust variance as φ·n_i·π̂_i·(1-π̂_i), which makes statistical tests more stringent by correcting for variance in excess of binomial expectations and automatically switches from Chisq to F-test.
inputs: methylKit package with dataSim() and calculateDiffMeth() functions available in R environment
methodology_summary: Generate synthetic methylation data using dataSim() with 6 replicates and 1000 genomic sites to create a methylBase object.
Run calculateDiffMeth() with overdispersion correction method set to MN and statistical test set to Chi-square.
Run calculateDiffMeth() again without overdispersion correction (baseline) on the same methylBase object.
Extract and tabulate q-value statistics from both runs and compare distributions.
Validation: Confirm that average q-values from the MN-corrected run are higher than the uncorrected baseline, confirming more stringent multiple-testing control.
research_question: Does overdispersion correction in calculateDiffMeth() with overdispersion='MN' produce more stringent statistical tests (higher q-values) compared to uncorrected differential methylation analysis?

task_description: Simulate a methylBase object using dataSim() with 6 replicates and 1000 methylation sites, then run calculateDiffMeth() with overdispersion correction (MN method) and Chi-square test. Verify that the function returns a methylDiff object and that the q-value distribution is more stringent (higher average q-values) compared to an uncorrected baseline run.

task_inputs.label: methylKit R package with dataSim and calculateDiffMeth functions

task_objective: Simulate methylation data with dataSim(), apply calculateDiffMeth() with overdispersion correction enabled, and verify the function returns a methylDiff object with statistically more stringent results than the uncorrected baseline.

task_outputs.label: methylDiff object with overdispersion-corrected test statistics

title: Reconstruct overdispersion-corrected differential methylation using dataSim and calculateDiffMeth with overdispersion='MN'

Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: methylDiff object containing differential methylation test statistics, p-values, and q-values from the overdispersion-corrected run, Comparison report or figure showing q-value distributions (corrected vs. uncorrected) demonstrating that MN-corrected q-values are higher on average.
workflow_description: 1. Generate a simulated methylBase object using methylKit's dataSim() function with parameters set to 6 replicates and 1000 methylation sites. 2. Execute calculateDiffMeth() on the methylBase object with overdispersion parameter set to 'MN' (Methylation-specific Negative Binomial) and test parameter set to 'Chisq' (Chi-square test). 3. Extract and compare the q-value distributions from the corrected run (overdispersion='MN') against a parallel uncorrected run (overdispersion=FALSE or default) to verify that the corrected method produces higher average q-values, indicating more stringent multiple-testing correction.
